plan.reasoning: Round 1 should establish the rigorous backbone and attack the real bottleneck. The central fiber/coarea argument is likely correct and reusable, but the proposed source filling lemma for arbitrary cycles is suspect: a product cylinder [0,R1] times a circle can have fill about S1 S2 S3 with area much smaller than S1 sqrt(S2 S3) in highly eccentric rectangles. Therefore I will ask one solver to formalize the fiber/coarea/degree reduction, and another to verify the local-lemma obstruction and seek a family-level Almgren/linking/solid-torus replacement.
plan.strategic_note: Use Guth's standard monomial estimates only as background; they do not see the sqrt(S3/S2) factor. The local arbitrary-cycle filling lemma is likely false, so future work should focus on the fact that the cycles are a 2-parameter family of degree-one fibers, not isolated cycles.

new_proven_results: Known tool from Guth: for an n-rectangle R with side lengths R1 <= ... <= Rn, the relative k-cycle filling profile satisfies: if V <= c R1...Rk and V = c R1...Rj rho^(k-j) with Rj <= rho <= R_{j+1}, then I_R^k(V) <= C R1...Rj rho^(k-j+1); in any case I_R^k(V) <= C R_{k+1} V.
Known tool from Guth estimates for n=4,k=2,D=1,Dil_2<=1: R1 R2 >= c S1 S2, R1 R2 R3 >= c S1 S2 S3, Vol(R) >= c Vol(S), R1^2 R2 R3 >= c S1^2 S2 S3, and R1^2 Vol(R) >= c S1^3 S2 S3 S4.
new_failed_attempts: The naive pointwise source lemma for arbitrary relative 2-cycles is likely false. In normalized variables S1=S2=1, S3=q, S4=L, one can choose a very thin R1 and large R2=R3 so that a relative cylinder [0,R1] times a circle in the x2x3-plane has filling volume about q but area o(sqrt(q)), while still satisfying the standard monomial estimates and the negations of the desired conclusions for suitable q,L.
new_bottlenecks: Need a family-level obstruction for the central fibers z_y = (pi_34 o f)^{-1}(y), probably via Almgren complexes, tightening, Sq_1/minimax, or a solid-torus/linking construction. Isolated filling-volume lower bounds are insufficient.
kb_updates.frontier: Prove that the 2-parameter family of central fibers has large average area, not merely that each fiber has large filling volume.

task_assignments.solver_prompt: You are Solver t1. Prove the rigorous central-fiber reduction for the following problem. Original problem: R and S are 4-dimensional rectangles with side lengths R1 <= R2 <= R3 <= R4 and S1 <= S2 <= S3 <= S4. A piecewise smooth degree-one map of pairs f:(R,partial R)->(S,partial S) has Dil_2(f) <= 1. If R1 <= k S1, prove for some dimensional k>0 that either R3 R4 > k S3 S4 or Vol(R) > k S1 S2^(1/2) S3^(3/2) S4. Your task is not to solve the full theorem, but to establish the fiber/coarea backbone with all relative-chain details. Let pi:S->[0,S3]x[0,S4] be projection to the last two target coordinates, and F=pi o f. For a regular y=(y3,y4) with y3 in the central interval [S3/3,2S3/3], define z_y=F^{-1}(y), oriented as a relative 2-current in (R,partial R), and P_y=[0,S1]x[0,S2]x{y3}x{y4} in S. Prove carefully: (1) z_y is a relative 2-cycle and f_# z_y equals P_y as a relative 2-chain in (S,partial S), possibly modulo chains in partial S; equivalently f_*[z_y]=[P_y] in H_2(P_y,partial P_y) or via cap/intersection products. (2) Any relative filling Y of z_y in R pushes forward to a relative filling of P_y in S. Since Dil_2(f)<=1 implies Dil_3(f)<=1, conclude FillVol_R(z_y) >= FillVol_S(P_y). (3) Prove FillVol_S(P_y) >= c S1 S2 S3 for y3 in [S3/3,2S3/3]. A calibration, slicing, or projection argument is acceptable; be explicit about relative boundary. (4) Prove the coarea estimate integral over [0,S3]x[0,S4] of Area(z_y) dy <= Vol(R), using Jac_2(pi o f) <= Dil_2(f). (5) Conclude a clean conditional proposition: if for a.e. central y one has Area(z_y) >= a S1 sqrt(S2 S3), then Vol(R) >= c a S1 S2^(1/2) S3^(3/2) S4. Also state the weaker version needed if the lower bound holds only on a fixed positive fraction of the central base. Handle piecewise smooth maps by either smoothing/approximation or integral-current language. Be precise and self-contained.

task_assignments.solver_prompt: You are Solver t2. Attack the hard part of the 4D 2-dilation rectangle problem. Original problem: R and S are 4-dimensional rectangles with side lengths R1 <= R2 <= R3 <= R4 and S1 <= S2 <= S3 <= S4. A piecewise smooth degree-one map of pairs f:(R,partial R)->(S,partial S) has Dil_2(f) <= 1. If R1 <= k S1, prove for some dimensional k>0 that either R3 R4 > k S3 S4 or Vol(R) > k S1 S2^(1/2) S3^(3/2) S4. You may use these known Guth tools: standard monomial estimates for this situation include R1 R2 >= c S1 S2, R1 R2 R3 >= c S1 S2 S3, Vol(R) >= c Vol(S), R1^2 R2 R3 >= c S1^2 S2 S3, and R1^2 Vol(R) >= c S1^3 S2 S3 S4. Also, the rectangle relative isoperimetric profile for 2-cycles says roughly: if area V is in the small range V ~ rho^2 with rho <= R1, fill <= C rho^3; if V ~ R1 rho with R1 <= rho <= R2, fill <= C R1 rho^2; and always fill <= C R3 V. Important warning: do not rely blindly on the naive pointwise lemma that every relative 2-cycle with fill >= c S1 S2 S3 has area >= c S1 sqrt(S2 S3). It appears false for arbitrary cycles. Please first verify or refute the following suspected counterexample to that pointwise lemma: normalize S1=S2=1, S3=q, S4=L. For small constants b,delta and q,L large, take r=(b^(1/4)/sqrt(delta)) q^(-1/4), t=sqrt(b) q^(1/2)/r, and set R1=r, R2=R3=t, R4=delta q L/t. Then R3 R4=delta q L, Vol(R)=delta sqrt(b) q^(3/2) L, R1^2 R2 R3=b q, and R1^2 Vol(R)=b q L. A relative cylinder [0,R1] times a circle of radius rho ~ sqrt(q/r) in the x2x3-plane has fill ~ q but area ~ sqrt(r q)=o(sqrt(q)), and it fits for q large. If this is valid, explain it succinctly and then move on. The real goal is to prove a family-level obstruction for the actual fibers z_y=(pi_34 o f)^(-1)(y), which form a 2-parameter degree-one family and satisfy FillVol_R(z_y) >= c S1 S2 S3 for central y. Try one or more of these approaches: (A) Almgren complex/tightening: assume the average fiber area is below c S1 sqrt(S2 S3), subdivide the target base, fill/tighten cells compatibly, and show the induced degree-one complex becomes null-homotopic, contradiction. (B) Linking/minimax/Sq_1: double R and S or formulate a relative version; build a test map on S with a second-order linking or Steenrod-square invariant tuned to scale S1 S2^(1/2) S3^(3/2) S4; compose with f and bound the invariant from the source under the negation of both desired alternatives. (C) Solid-torus mechanism: adapt Guth's 3-dimensional solid-torus argument to the 4-dimensional relative family, crossing with the S1 direction. Deliver the strongest result you can: ideally a complete proof of the original theorem; otherwise a precise family lemma with proof and a clear statement of any remaining gap. Avoid merely rederiving the standard monomial estimates.

task_assignments.description: Direct proof of the base-stretch dispersion lemma
task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are Solver t3. Your goal is to prove, or sharply test, the base-stretch dispersion lemma identified in t2. The full outputs of t1 and t2 are attached; use their notation and do not redo their ruled-out pointwise arguments.

Work under the contradiction hypotheses with a small dimensional epsilon: R1 <= epsilon S1, R3 R4 <= epsilon S3 S4, and Vol(R) <= epsilon S1 S2^(1/2) S3^(3/2) S4. Let F=(f3,f4): R -> [0,S3]x[0,S4], G=(f1,f2), and let B0 be the doubly central base [S3/3,2S3/3] x [S4/3,2S4/3]. At regular points over B0, let σ1 >= σ2 be the singular values of dF on the normal 2-plane to the fiber z_y=F^{-1}(y), and define dμ=(σ2/σ1) dx.

Known from t2: μ(F^{-1}(B0)) >= c S1 S2 S3 S4, and ∫_{F^{-1}(B0)} σ1^2 dμ = ∫_{B0} Area(z_y) dy <= Vol(R). The theorem would follow from ∫ σ1^2 dμ >= c sqrt(S3/S2) μ(F^{-1}(B0)). Try to prove this estimate, with constants depending only on dimension, from the actual map hypotheses and the side assumptions.

Suggested avenues: derive a distributional estimate for μ({σ1 <= L}); combine the mixed Jacobian inequality J2(G restricted to fibers) <= σ1^{-2} with the degree-one property of G on fibers; use slicing, directional coarea, the rectangular isoperimetric profile, or width estimates for the sublevel region {σ1 <= L}; exploit R1 small and R3 R4 small in an essential way. If a full proof is out of reach, isolate a precise weaker lemma that would still imply the theorem. If you believe the dispersion lemma as stated is false, construct a genuine counterexample satisfying degree one, boundary-to-boundary, and Dil_2 <= 1, not merely an arbitrary sweepout family.

## Task t1 — Rigorous central-fiber/coarea reduction
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Suppose $(M, g)$ and $(N,h)$ are piecewise smooth Riemannian manifolds.   If $f: M \rightarrow N$ is piecewise smooth,  we define the $k$-dilation of $f$ as the infimal $\Lambda$ so that for every $k$-dimensional submanifold $\Sigma \subset M$, 

$$ \Vol_k( f(\Sigma) ) \le \Lambda \Vol_k (\Sigma). $$

\noindent We write the $k$-dilation of $f$ as $\Dil_k(f)$.   Equivalently,  $\Dil_k(f) = \sup_{x \in M} \| \Lambda^k df \|$.

Prove that there is a constant $k > 0$ so that the following holds.

Suppose that $R \subset \RR^4$ is a 4-dimensional rectangle with side lengths $R_1 \le R_2 \le R_3 \le R_4$ and $S$ is a 4-dimensional rectangle with side lengths $S_1 \le S_2 \le S_3 \le S_4$.   Suppose that $f: (R, \partial R) \rightarrow (S, \partial S)$ is a piecewise smooth map with degree 1 and with $\Dil_2(f) \le 1$.   Suppose that $R_1 \le k S_1$.   Then either

$$ R_3 R_4 > k S_3 S_4,  or $$

$$ R_1 R_2 R_3 R_4 > k S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4. $$]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Below, \(\llbracket A\rrbracket\) denotes the integral current carried by an oriented rectangle \(A\). I will use the integral-current model for relative chains:
\[
T\in Z_k(M,\partial M)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \partial T\text{ is supported in }\partial M,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_M(T)=\inf\{\mathbf M(Y):\ Y\in \mathbf I_{k+1}(M),\ \partial Y-T\in \mathbf I_k(\partial M)\}.
\]

There is one necessary correction: item **(3)** as stated is false if only \(y_3\) is central. A slice \(P_y\) with \(y_4\) very close to \(0\) can be filled by a thin prism to the face \(x_4=0\). The correct target estimate is
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)=S_1S_2\,\operatorname{dist}\bigl((y_3,y_4),\partial([0,S_3]\times[0,S_4])\bigr).
\]
Thus the desired lower bound \( \gtrsim S_1S_2S_3\) holds on the doubly central sub-base, hence on a fixed positive fraction of the \(y_3\)-central base.

## 1. The fiber is a relative cycle and pushes to \(P_y\)

Let
\[
B=[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4],\qquad \pi:S\to B,\qquad F=\pi\circ f.
\]
Assume first that \(y\in \operatorname{int}B\) is a good regular value of \(F\), i.e. regular on all smooth strata of \(R\) and \(\partial R\). Then
\[
z_y:=F^{-1}(y)
\]
is an oriented compact surface with boundary contained in \(\partial R\). Equivalently, as a current,
\[
\partial z_y\in \mathbf I_1(\partial R),
\]
so \(z_y\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \((R,\partial R)\).

Because \(F=\pi\circ f\), every point of \(z_y\) maps into
\[
\pi^{-1}(y)=P_y=[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y_3\}\times\{y_4\}.
\]
Also \(f(\partial R)\subset \partial S\), so
\[
\operatorname{spt}\partial(f_\#z_y)\subset P_y\cap\partial S=\partial P_y.
\]
Thus \(f_\#z_y\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \((P_y,\partial P_y)\).

Now let \(u_y\in H^2(B,B\setminus\{y\};\mathbb Z)\) be the local orientation class of \(B\) at \(y\). By Poincare-Lefschetz cap product and excision,
\[
[z_y]=[R,\partial R]\frown F^*u_y.
\]
Since \(F=\pi\circ f\) and \(f\) has degree one as a map of pairs,
\[
\begin{aligned}
f_*[z_y]
&=f_*\bigl([R,\partial R]\frown f^*\pi^*u_y\bigr)\\
&=f_*[R,\partial R]\frown \pi^*u_y\\
&=[S,\partial S]\frown \pi^*u_y\\
&=[P_y,\partial P_y].
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore \(f_\#z_y\) represents the fundamental relative class of \(P_y\).

In current language this gives actual equality. Indeed, \(f_\#z_y\) is an integral \(2\)-current supported in the connected \(2\)-rectangle \(P_y\), and its boundary is supported in \(\partial P_y\). By the constancy theorem,
\[
f_\#z_y=m\,\llbracket P_y\rrbracket
\]
for some \(m\in\mathbb Z\). The cap-product computation gives \(m=1\). Hence
\[
f_\#z_y=\llbracket P_y\rrbracket
\]
as an integral current in \(S\), and therefore certainly as a relative chain modulo \(\partial S\).

## 2. Fillings push forward, and \(\operatorname{Dil}_2\le 1\) gives the filling inequality

Let \(Y\in \mathbf I_3(R)\) be a relative filling of \(z_y\), so
\[
\partial Y-z_y\in \mathbf I_2(\partial R).
\]
Pushing forward,
\[
\partial(f_\#Y)-f_\#z_y=f_\#(\partial Y-z_y)
\]
is supported in \(f(\partial R)\subset\partial S\). Since \(f_\#z_y=P_y\), we get
\[
\partial(f_\#Y)-P_y\in \mathbf I_2(\partial S).
\]
Thus \(f_\#Y\) is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\).

At a differentiability point of \(f\), let
\[
\sigma_1\ge \sigma_2\ge \sigma_3\ge \sigma_4\ge 0
\]
be the singular values of \(df\). The assumption \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\) means \(\sigma_1\sigma_2\le 1\). Hence \(\sigma_2\le 1\), so also \(\sigma_3\le 1\), and therefore
\[
\sigma_1\sigma_2\sigma_3\le 1.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\le 1\). Consequently,
\[
\mathbf M(f_\#Y)\le \operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\,\mathbf M(Y)\le \mathbf M(Y).
\]
Taking the infimum over all relative fillings \(Y\) of \(z_y\),
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_y)\ge \operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y).
\]

## 3. Correct filling estimate in \(S\)

Let
\[
\delta(y):=\operatorname{dist}\bigl(y,\partial B\bigr)
=\min\{y_3,S_3-y_3,y_4,S_4-y_4\}.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)=S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]

For the lower bound, choose a smooth function \(\rho:B\to\mathbb R\) with \(\rho|_{\partial B}=0\), \(|\nabla \rho|\le 1+\varepsilon\), and \(\rho(y)\ge \delta(y)-\varepsilon\). Define the \(2\)-form
\[
\alpha=\rho(x_3,x_4)\,dx_1\wedge dx_2.
\]
This form restricts to \(0\) on every face of \(\partial S\): on the \(x_1,x_2\)-faces because \(dx_1\wedge dx_2\) vanishes there, and on the \(x_3,x_4\)-faces because \(\rho=0\) on \(\partial B\).

If \(Y\) is a relative filling of \(P_y\), then \(\partial Y-P_y\) is supported in \(\partial S\), so Stokes gives
\[
\int_Y d\alpha=\int_{\partial Y}\alpha=\int_{P_y}\alpha=\rho(y)S_1S_2.
\]
Moreover
\[
d\alpha=d\rho\wedge dx_1\wedge dx_2
\]
has comass at most \(1+\varepsilon\). Hence
\[
(\delta(y)-\varepsilon)S_1S_2\le (1+\varepsilon)\mathbf M(Y).
\]
Letting \(\varepsilon\to 0\) yields
\[
\mathbf M(Y)\ge S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]

For the upper bound, take a shortest segment \(\gamma\) in \(B\) from \(y\) to \(\partial B\). Then
\[
[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times \gamma
\]
is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\), with mass \(S_1S_2\,\ell(\gamma)=S_1S_2\,\delta(y)\).

So the formula is exact.

In particular, if
\[
y_3\in[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\quad\text{and}\quad y_4\in[S_4/3,2S_4/3],
\]
then, because \(S_4\ge S_3\),
\[
\delta(y)\ge S_3/3,
\]
and hence
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)\ge \frac13 S_1S_2S_3.
\]

But with only \(y_3\) central, no such uniform lower bound is true. Indeed, if \(y_4=\varepsilon\), then the prism
\[
[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y_3\}\times[0,\varepsilon]
\]
fills \(P_y\) relative to \(\partial S\) and has mass \(S_1S_2\varepsilon\).

## 4. Coarea estimate

For a.e. \(y\in B\), the slice \(z_y=\langle \llbracket R\rrbracket,F,y\rangle\) exists, and for good regular values it agrees with the smooth fiber \(F^{-1}(y)\). The coarea formula gives
\[
\int_B \operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_R \operatorname{Jac}_2 F(x)\,dx.
\]
Since \(F=\pi\circ f\) and \(\pi\) is an orthogonal projection,
\[
\operatorname{Jac}_2 F(x)=|\Lambda^2(d\pi\circ df_x)|
\le |\Lambda^2 df_x|

## 1. The suspected pointwise counterexample is valid

Normalize \(S_1=S_2=1,\ S_3=q,\ S_4=L\). With

\[
r=b^{1/4}\delta^{-1/2}q^{-1/4},\qquad 
t=\frac{\sqrt b\,q^{1/2}}r=b^{1/4}\delta^{1/2}q^{3/4},
\]

and

\[
R_1=r,\quad R_2=R_3=t,\quad R_4=\frac{\delta qL}{t},
\]

we get

\[
R_3R_4=\delta qL,
\]

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)=r\cdot t\cdot t\cdot \frac{\delta qL}{t}
=\delta\sqrt b\,q^{3/2}L,
\]

\[
R_1^2R_2R_3=r^2t^2=bq,
\]

and

\[
R_1^2\operatorname{Vol}(R)=bqL.
\]

For \(q\) large, \(R_1\le R_2=R_3\), and for \(L\gtrsim b^{1/2}q^{1/2}\), also \(R_3\le R_4\).

Now take a circle \(\gamma\) of radius

\[
\rho \sim \sqrt{\frac q r}
\]

in the \(x_2x_3\)-plane, centered far from the boundary. Since

\[
\frac{t}{\rho}
\sim b^{3/8}\delta^{1/4}q^{1/8}\to\infty,
\]

the circle fits for \(q\) large. Let

\[
z=[0,r]\times \gamma
\]

at a fixed central \(x_4\). Then \(z\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle: its boundary lies in the faces \(x_1=0,r\). Its area is

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z)\sim r\rho
=\sqrt{rq}
=b^{1/8}\delta^{-1/4}q^{3/8}
=o(\sqrt q).
\]

But the natural filling \([0,r]\times D_\gamma\) has volume

\[
\sim r\rho^2\sim q.
\]

A matching lower bound follows by slicing any relative filling \(Y\) by \(x_1=s\). For a.e. \(s\), the slice \(Y_s\) is a relative \(2\)-chain in the \(x_2x_3x_4\)-box filling \(\gamma\). Since \(\gamma\) sits a distance \(\gtrsim \rho\) from the boundary, any such relative filling has area \(\gtrsim \rho^2\). Hence

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(Y)\ge \int_0^r c\rho^2\,ds\gtrsim r\rho^2\sim q.
\]

So the cylinder has

\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z)\sim q=S_1S_2S_3
\]

but

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z)\sim q^{3/8}\ll S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}=\sqrt q.
\]

Thus the naive pointwise lemma is false.

## 2. A pure Almgren-family lemma is also insufficient

Even adding “the cycles form a degree-one \(2\)-parameter family” is not enough unless one uses the fact that the family comes from an actual \(2\)-dilation map.

Here is a model family satisfying the numerical side constraints and the central filling lower bound, but with average area far below \(\sqrt q\).

Take \(S=(1,1,q,q)\), choose small \(\kappa\), and set

\[
R_1=\kappa,\qquad 
R_2=\kappa^{-4},\qquad 
R_3=q\kappa^2,\qquad 
R_4=\frac q\kappa,
\]

with \(q\ge \kappa^{-8}\). Then

\[
R_3R_4=q^2\kappa=\kappa S_3S_4,
\]

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)=\frac{q^2}{\kappa^2}
\le \kappa q^{5/2}
=\kappa S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]

for \(q^{1/2}\ge \kappa^{-3}\). Also the standard monomial estimates are saturated or satisfied:

\[
R_1R_2=\kappa^{-3}\ge 1,

\[
R_1R_2R_3=\frac q\kappa\ge q,

\[
R_1^2R_2R_3=q,
\]

\[
R_1^2\operatorname{Vol}(R)=q^2=S_1^3S_2S_3S_4.

Define a family of relative \(2\)-cycles parameterized by \((u,v)\in [0,q]^2\):

\[
z_{u,v}=[0,R_1]\times [0,R_2]\times 
\left\{\frac{R_3}{q}u\right\}
\times
\left\{\frac{R_4}{q}v\right\}.

This is an Almgren degree-one sweepout of \(R\). Each cycle has area

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z_{u,v})=R_1R_2=\kappa^{-3}.
\]

For central \((u,v)\), the distance in the \(x_3x_4\)-rectangle to the boundary is \(\gtrsim R_3\). A double slicing argument gives

\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_{u,v})
\gtrsim R_1R_2R_3
=\frac q\kappa
\gtrsim q.
\]

Indeed, slice a filling first by \(x_1\), then slice the resulting \(2\)-chain by \(x_2\). Each \(x_2\)-slice must connect the point \((x_3,x_4)\) to the boundary of the \(x_3x_4\)-rectangle, so has length \(\gtrsim R_3\). Integrating gives the lower bound.

\[

\]

Therefore no argument depending only on:

1. Almgren degree-one family,
2. central filling lower bound,
3. the standard monomial side estimates,

can prove the desired theorem. The proof must use the extra structure that the family is made of actual fibers of a degree-one map with \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\).

## 3. A structural lemma for actual fibers

Let

\[
F=(f_3,f_4):R\to [0,S_3]\times[0,S_4],
\qquad 
G=(f_1,f_2):R\to [0,S_1]\times[0,S_2].
\]

For a regular value \(y\), the fiber

\[
z_y=F^{-1}(y)
\]

is a relative \(2\)-cycle, and \(G|_{z_y}\) has relative degree one onto the \(S_1\times S_2\) fiber. Hence

\[
\int_{z_y} J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\,dA \ge S_1S_2.
\]

At a regular point \(x\in z_y\), let

\[
\sigma_1(x)\ge \sigma_2(x)>0

be the singular values of \(dF_x\) on \(T_xz_y^\perp\). Then the \(2\)-dilation bound implies

\[
J_2(G|_{T_xz_y})\le \sigma_1(x)^{-2}.
\]

Thus \(|dG(v)|\le \sigma_1^{-1}\), so the \(2\)-Jacobian of \(G\) on the fiber tangent plane is at most \(\sigma_1^{-2}\).

Therefore, for every regular central fiber,

\[
\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA \ge S_1S_2.
\]

Coarea gives

\[
\int_{B_0}\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\sigma_1^{-2}J_2F\,dx
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx,
\]

where \(B_0\subset [0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]\) is a fixed central subrectangle. Hence

\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx
\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Meanwhile,

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}J_2F\,dx
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1\sigma_2\,dx.
\]

Writing

\[
d\mu=\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx,
\]

we have

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu,
\]

and

\[
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0))
\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Thus the original theorem would follow from the following precise missing estimate:

\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu
\gtrsim
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}\,
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0)).
\]

Equivalently, the actual fibers must carry enough average maximal base-stretch:

\[
\langle \sigma_1^2\rangle_{\mu}
\gtrsim
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}.
\]

If this holds, then

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\gtrsim
S_1S_2S_3S_4
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}
=
S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]

and since

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\le \operatorname{Vol}(R),
\]

we get the desired volume lower bound.

So the hard remaining point is not a pointwise isoperimetric statement about arbitrary cycles. It is a mixed-Jacobian / solid-torus statement forcing the base map \(F=(f_3,f_4)\) to have enough \(\mu\)-average maximal stretch on the degree-carrying part of the fibers. The abstract counterfamilies above evade this because they do not come with coordinate functions \(f_1,f_2,f_3,f_4\) satisfying all mixed \(2\)-Jacobian bounds.

\pi^{-1}(y)=P_y=[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y_3\}\times\{y_4\}.

Also \(f(\partial R)\subset \partial S\), so
\[
\operatorname{spt}\partial(f_\#z_y)\subset P_y\cap\partial S=\partial P_y.
\]
Thus \(f_\#z_y\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \((P_y,\partial P_y)\).

Now let \(u_y\in H^2(B,B\setminus\{y\};\mathbb Z)\) be the local orientation class of \(B\) at \(y\). By Poincare-Lefschetz cap product and excision,
\[
[z_y]=[R,\partial R]\frown F^*u_y.
\]
Since \(F=\pi\circ f\) and \(f\) has degree one as a map of pairs,
\[
\begin{aligned}
f_*[z_y]
&=f_*\bigl([R,\partial R]\frown f^*\pi^*u_y\bigr)\\
&=f_*[R,\partial R]\frown \pi^*u_y\\
&=[S,\partial S]\frown \pi^*u_y\\
&=[P_y,\partial P_y].
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore \(f_\#z_y\) represents the fundamental relative class of \(P_y\).

In current language this gives actual equality. Indeed, \(f_\#z_y\) is an integral \(2\)-current supported in the connected \(2\)-rectangle \(P_y\), and its boundary is supported in \(\partial P_y\). By the constancy theorem,
\[
f_\#z_y=m\,\llbracket P_y\rrbracket
\]
for some \(m\in\mathbb Z\). The cap-product computation gives \(m=1\). Hence
\[
f_\#z_y=\llbracket P_y\rrbracket
\]
as an integral current in \(S\), and therefore certainly as a relative chain modulo \(\partial S\).

## 2. Fillings push forward, and \(\operatorname{Dil}_2\le 1\) gives the filling inequality

Let \(Y\in \mathbf I_3(R)\) be a relative filling of \(z_y\), so
\[
\partial Y-z_y\in \mathbf I_2(\partial R).
\]
Pushing forward,
\[
\partial(f_\#Y)-f_\#z_y=f_\#(\partial Y-z_y)
\]
is supported in \(f(\partial R)\subset\partial S\). Since \(f_\#z_y=P_y\), we get
\[
\partial(f_\#Y)-P_y\in \mathbf I_2(\partial S).
\]
Thus \(f_\#Y\) is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\).

At a differentiability point of \(f\), let
\[
\sigma_1\ge \sigma_2\ge \sigma_3\ge \sigma_4\ge 0
\]
be the singular values of \(df\). The assumption \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\) means \(\sigma_1\sigma_2\le 1\). Hence \(\sigma_2\le 1\), so also \(\sigma_3\le 1\), and therefore
\[
\sigma_1\sigma_2\sigma_3\le 1.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\le 1\). Consequently,
\[
\mathbf M(f_\#Y)\le \operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\,\mathbf M(Y)\le \mathbf M(Y).
\]
Taking the infimum over all relative fillings \(Y\) of \(z_y\),
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_y)\ge \operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y).
\]

## 3. Correct filling estimate in \(S\)

Let
\[
\delta(y):=\operatorname{dist}\bigl(y,\partial B\bigr)
=\min\{y_3,S_3-y_3,y_4,S_4-y_4\}.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)=S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]

For the lower bound, choose a smooth function \(\rho:B\to\mathbb R\) with \(\rho|_{\partial B}=0\), \(|\nabla \rho|\le 1+\varepsilon\), and \(\rho(y)\ge \delta(y)-\varepsilon\). Define the \(2\)-form
\[
\alpha=\rho(x_3,x_4)\,dx_1\wedge dx_2.
\]
This form restricts to \(0\) on every face of \(\partial S\): on the \(x_1,x_2\)-faces because \(dx_1\wedge dx_2\) vanishes there, and on the \(x_3,x_4\)-faces because \(\rho=0\) on \(\partial B\).

If \(Y\) is a relative filling of \(P_y\), then \(\partial Y-P_y\) is supported in \(\partial S\), so Stokes gives
\[
\int_Y d\alpha=\int_{\partial Y}\alpha=\int_{P_y}\alpha=\rho(y)S_1S_2.
\]
Moreover
\[
d\alpha=d\rho\wedge dx_1\wedge dx_2
\]
has comass at most \(1+\varepsilon\). Hence
\[
(\delta(y)-\varepsilon)S_1S_2\le (1+\varepsilon)\mathbf M(Y).
\]
Letting \(\varepsilon\to 0\) yields
\[
\mathbf M(Y)\ge S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]

For the upper bound, take a shortest segment \(\gamma\) in \(B\) from \(y\) to \(\partial B\). Then
\[
[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times \gamma
\]
is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\), with mass \(S_1S_2\,\ell(\gamma)=S_1S_2\,\delta(y)\).

So the formula is exact.

In particular, if
\[
y_3\in[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\quad\text{and}\quad y_4\in[S_4/3,2S_4/3],
\]
then, because \(S_4\ge S_3\),
\[
\delta(y)\ge S_3/3,
\]
and hence
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)\ge \frac13 S_1S_2S_3.
\]

But with only \(y_3\) central, no such uniform lower bound is true. Indeed, if \(y_4=\varepsilon\), then the prism
\[
[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y_3\}\times[0,\varepsilon]
\]
fills \(P_y\) relative to \(\partial S\) and has mass \(S_1S_2\varepsilon\).

## 4. Coarea estimate

For a.e. \(y\in B\), the slice \(z_y=\langle \llbracket R\rrbracket,F,y\rangle\) exists, and for good regular values it agrees with the smooth fiber \(F^{-1}(y)\). The coarea formula gives
\[
\int_B \operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_R \operatorname{Jac}_2 F(x)\,dx.
\]
Since \(F=\pi\circ f\) and \(\pi\) is an orthogonal projection,
\[
\operatorname{Jac}_2 F(x)=|\Lambda^2(d\pi\circ df_x)|
\le |\Lambda^2 df_x|
\le \operatorname{Dil}_2(f)
\le 1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\int_{[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\le \operatorname{Vol}(R).
\]

## 5. Conditional central-base proposition

Let
\[
B_c=[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\times[0,S_4].
\]
If for a.e. \(y\in B_c\),
\[
\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\ge a\,S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3},
\]
then by coarea,
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)
\ge \int_{B_c}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\ge aS_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}\cdot \frac{S_3S_4}{3}.
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)\ge \frac a3\,S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]

More generally, if the same area lower bound holds on a measurable subset \(E\subset B_c\) with
\[
|E|\ge \theta |B_c|
\]
for some fixed \(\theta>0\), then
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)\ge \frac{\theta a}{3}\,S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]
For example, the doubly central base
\[
[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\times[S_4/3,2S_4/3]
\]
has \(\theta=1/3\) relative to \(B_c\).

Piecewise smooth maps are handled directly in this current framework: slices exist for a.e. \(y\), the boundary and pushforward identities hold for good regular values on the smooth strata, and coarea is obtained by summing over the finitely many smooth pieces. No smoothing is needed.

## 1. The suspected pointwise counterexample is valid

Normalize \(S_1=S_2=1,\ S_3=q,\ S_4=L\). With

\[
r=b^{1/4}\delta^{-1/2}q^{-1/4},\qquad 
t=\frac{\sqrt b\,q^{1/2}}r=b^{1/4}\delta^{1/2}q^{3/4},
\]

and

\[
R_1=r,\quad R_2=R_3=t,\quad R_4=\frac{\delta qL}{t},
\]

we get

\[
R_3R_4=\delta qL,
\]

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)=r\cdot t\cdot t\cdot \frac{\delta qL}{t}
=\delta\sqrt b\,q^{3/2}L,
\]

\[
R_1^2R_2R_3=r^2t^2=bq,
\]

and

\[
R_1^2\operatorname{Vol}(R)=bqL.
\]

For \(q\) large, \(R_1\le R_2=R_3\), and for \(L\gtrsim b^{1/2}q^{1/2}\), also \(R_3\le R_4\).

Now take a circle \(\gamma\) of radius

\[
\rho \sim \sqrt{\frac q r}
\]

in the \(x_2x_3\)-plane, centered far from the boundary. Since

\[
\frac{t}{\rho}
\sim b^{3/8}\delta^{1/4}q^{1/8}\to\infty,
\]

the circle fits for \(q\) large. Let

\[
z=[0,r]\times \gamma
\]

at a fixed central \(x_4\). Then \(z\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle: its boundary lies in the faces \(x_1=0,r\). Its area is

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z)\sim r\rho
=\sqrt{rq}
=b^{1/8}\delta^{-1/4}q^{3/8}
=o(\sqrt q).
\]

But the natural filling \([0,r]\times D_\gamma\) has volume

\[
\sim r\rho^2\sim q.
\]

A matching lower bound follows by slicing any relative filling \(Y\) by \(x_1=s\). For a.e. \(s\), the slice \(Y_s\) is a relative \(2\)-chain in the \(x_2x_3x_4\)-box filling \(\gamma\). Since \(\gamma\) sits a distance \(\gtrsim \rho\) from the boundary, any such relative filling has area \(\gtrsim \rho^2\). Hence

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(Y)\ge \int_0^r c\rho^2\,ds\gtrsim r\rho^2\sim q.
\]

So the cylinder has

\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z)\sim q=S_1S_2S_3
\]

but

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z)\sim q^{3/8}\ll S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}=\sqrt q.
\]

Thus the naive pointwise lemma is false.

---

## 2. A pure Almgren-family lemma is also insufficient

Even adding “the cycles form a degree-one \(2\)-parameter family” is not enough unless one uses the fact that the family comes from an actual \(2\)-dilation map.

Here is a model family satisfying the numerical side constraints and the central filling lower bound, but with average area far below \(\sqrt q\).

Take \(S=(1,1,q,q)\), choose small \(\kappa\), and set

\[
R_1=\kappa,\qquad 
R_2=\kappa^{-4},\qquad 
R_3=q\kappa^2,\qquad 
R_4=\frac q\kappa,
\]

with \(q\ge \kappa^{-8}\). Then

\[
R_3R_4=q^2\kappa=\kappa S_3S_4,
\]

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)=\frac{q^2}{\kappa^2}
\le \kappa q^{5/2}
=\kappa S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]

for \(q^{1/2}\ge \kappa^{-3}\). Also the standard monomial estimates are saturated or satisfied:

\[
R_1R_2=\kappa^{-3}\ge 1,
\]

\[
R_1R_2R_3=\frac q\kappa\ge q,
\]

\[
R_1^2R_2R_3=q,
\]

\[
R_1^2\operatorname{Vol}(R)=q^2=S_1^3S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Define a family of relative \(2\)-cycles parameterized by \((u,v)\in [0,q]^2\):

\[
z_{u,v}=[0,R_1]\times [0,R_2]\times 
\left\{\frac{R_3}{q}u\right\}
\times
\left\{\frac{R_4}{q}v\right\}.
\]

This is an Almgren degree-one sweepout of \(R\). Each cycle has area

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z_{u,v})=R_1R_2=\kappa^{-3}.
\]

For central \((u,v)\), the distance in the \(x_3x_4\)-rectangle to the boundary is \(\gtrsim R_3\). A double slicing argument gives

\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_{u,v})
\gtrsim R_1R_2R_3
=\frac q\kappa
\gtrsim q.
\]

Indeed, slice a filling first by \(x_1\), then slice the resulting \(2\)-chain by \(x_2\). Each \(x_2\)-slice must connect the point \((x_3,x_4)\) to the boundary of the \(x_3x_4\)-rectangle, so has length \(\gtrsim R_3\). Integrating gives the lower bound.

But for \(q\gg \kappa^{-6}\),

\[
\kappa^{-3}\ll \sqrt q.
\]

Therefore no argument depending only on:

1. Almgren degree-one family,
2. central filling lower bound,
3. the standard monomial side estimates,

can prove the desired theorem. The proof must use the extra structure that the family is made of actual fibers of a degree-one map with \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\).

---

## 3. A structural lemma for actual fibers

Let

\[
F=(f_3,f_4):R\to [0,S_3]\times[0,S_4],
\qquad 
G=(f_1,f_2):R\to [0,S_1]\times[0,S_2].
\]

For a regular value \(y\), the fiber

\[
z_y=F^{-1}(y)
\]

is a relative \(2\)-cycle, and \(G|_{z_y}\) has relative degree one onto the \(S_1\times S_2\) fiber. Hence

\[
\int_{z_y} J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\,dA \ge S_1S_2.
\]

At a regular point \(x\in z_y\), let

\[
\sigma_1(x)\ge \sigma_2(x)>0
\]

be the singular values of \(dF_x\) on \(T_xz_y^\perp\). Then the \(2\)-dilation bound implies

\[
J_2(G|_{T_xz_y})\le \sigma_1(x)^{-2}.
\]

Proof: choose a unit normal vector \(n\) with \(|dF(n)|=\sigma_1\). For every unit \(v\in T_xz_y\), we have \(dF(v)=0\). The \(G(v)\wedge F(n)\) component of \(df(v)\wedge df(n)\) has norm \(|dG(v)|\sigma_1\). Since \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\), this is \(\le 1\). Thus \(|dG(v)|\le \sigma_1^{-1}\), so the \(2\)-Jacobian of \(G\) on the fiber tangent plane is at most \(\sigma_1^{-2}\).

Therefore, for every regular central fiber,

\[
\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA \ge S_1S_2.
\]

Coarea gives

\[
\int_{B_0}\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\sigma_1^{-2}J_2F\,dx
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx,
\]

where \(B_0\subset [0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]\) is a fixed central subrectangle. Hence

\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx
\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Meanwhile,

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}J_2F\,dx
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1\sigma_2\,dx.
\]

Writing

\[
d\mu=\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx,
\]

we have

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu,
\]

and

\[
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0))
\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Thus the original theorem would follow from the following precise missing estimate:

\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu
\gtrsim
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}\,
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0)).
\]

Equivalently, the actual fibers must carry enough average maximal base-stretch:

\[
\langle \sigma_1^2\rangle_{\mu}
\gtrsim
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}.
\]

If this holds, then

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\gtrsim
S_1S_2S_3S_4
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}
=
S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]

and since

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\le \operatorname{Vol}(R),
\]

we get the desired volume lower bound.

So the hard remaining point is not a pointwise isoperimetric statement about arbitrary cycles. It is a mixed-Jacobian / solid-torus statement forcing the base map \(F=(f_3,f_4)\) to have enough \(\mu\)-average maximal stretch on the degree-carrying part of the fibers. The abstract counterfamilies above evade this because they do not come with coordinate functions \(f_1,f_2,f_3,f_4\) satisfying all mixed \(2\)-Jacobian bounds.

$$ \Vol_k( f(\Sigma) ) \le \Lambda \Vol_k (\Sigma). $$

Prove that there is a constant $k > 0$ so that the following holds.

Below, \(\llbracket A\rrbracket\) denotes the integral current carried by an oriented rectangle \(A\). I will use the integral-current model for relative chains:
\[
T\in Z_k(M,\partial M)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \partial T\text{ is supported in }\partial M,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_M(T)=\inf\{\mathbf M(Y):\ Y\in \mathbf I_{k+1}(M),\ \partial Y-T\in \mathbf I_k(\partial M)\}.
\]

There is one necessary correction: item **(3)** as stated is false if only \(y_3\) is central. A slice \(P_y\) with \(y_4\) very close to \(0\) can be filled by a thin prism to the face \(x_4=0\). The correct target estimate is
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)=S_1S_2\,\operatorname{dist}\bigl((y_3,y_4),\partial([0,S_3]\times[0,S_4])\bigr).
\]
Thus the desired lower bound \( \gtrsim S_1S_2S_3\) holds on the doubly central sub-base, hence on a fixed positive fraction of the \(y_3\)-central base.

## 1. The fiber is a relative cycle and pushes to \(P_y\)

Let
\[
B=[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4],\qquad \pi:S\to B,\qquad F=\pi\circ f.
\]
Assume first that \(y\in \operatorname{int}B\) is a good regular value of \(F\), i.e. regular on all smooth strata of \(R\) and \(\partial R\). Then
\[
z_y:=F^{-1}(y)
\]
is an oriented compact surface with boundary contained in \(\partial R\). Equivalently, as a current,
\[
\partial z_y\in \mathbf I_1(\partial R),
\]
so \(z_y\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \((R,\partial R)\).

Because \(F=\pi\circ f\), every point of \(z_y\) maps into
\[
\pi^{-1}(y)=P_y=[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y_3\}\times\{y_4\}.
\]
Also \(f(\partial R)\subset \partial S\), so
\[
\operatorname{spt}\partial(f_\#z_y)\subset P_y\cap\partial S=\partial P_y.
\]
Thus \(f_\#z_y\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \((P_y,\partial P_y)\).

Now let \(u_y\in H^2(B,B\setminus\{y\};\mathbb Z)\) be the local orientation class of \(B\) at \(y\). By Poincare-Lefschetz cap product and excision,
\[
[z_y]=[R,\partial R]\frown F^*u_y.
\]
Since \(F=\pi\circ f\) and \(f\) has degree one as a map of pairs,
\[
\begin{aligned}
f_*[z_y]
&=f_*\bigl([R,\partial R]\frown f^*\pi^*u_y\bigr)\\
&=f_*[R,\partial R]\frown \pi^*u_y\\
&=[S,\partial S]\frown \pi^*u_y\\
&=[P_y,\partial P_y].
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore \(f_\#z_y\) represents the fundamental relative class of \(P_y\).

In current language this gives actual equality. Indeed, \(f_\#z_y\) is an integral \(2\)-current supported in the connected \(2\)-rectangle \(P_y\), and its boundary is supported in \(\partial P_y\). By the constancy theorem,
\[
f_\#z_y=m\,\llbracket P_y\rrbracket
\]
for some \(m\in\mathbb Z\). The cap-product computation gives \(m=1\). Hence
\[
f_\#z_y=\llbracket P_y\rrbracket
\]
as an integral current in \(S\), and therefore certainly as a relative chain modulo \(\partial S\).

## 2. Fillings push forward, and \(\operatorname{Dil}_2\le 1\) gives the filling inequality

Let \(Y\in \mathbf I_3(R)\) be a relative filling of \(z_y\), so
\[
\partial Y-z_y\in \mathbf I_2(\partial R).
\]
Pushing forward,
\[
\partial(f_\#Y)-f_\#z_y=f_\#(\partial Y-z_y)
\]
is supported in \(f(\partial R)\subset\partial S\). Since \(f_\#z_y=P_y\), we get
\[
\partial(f_\#Y)-P_y\in \mathbf I_2(\partial S).
\]
Thus \(f_\#Y\) is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\).

At a differentiability point of \(f\), let
\[
\sigma_1\ge \sigma_2\ge \sigma_3\ge \sigma_4\ge 0
\]
be the singular values of \(df\). The assumption \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\) means \(\sigma_1\sigma_2\le 1\). Hence \(\sigma_2\le 1\), so also \(\sigma_3\le 1\), and therefore
\[
\sigma_1\sigma_2\sigma_3\le 1.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\le 1\). Consequently,
\[
\mathbf M(f_\#Y)\le \operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\,\mathbf M(Y)\le \mathbf M(Y).
\]
Taking the infimum over all relative fillings \(Y\) of \(z_y\),
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_y)\ge \operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y).
\]

## 3. Correct filling estimate in \(S\)

Let
\[
\delta(y):=\operatorname{dist}\bigl(y,\partial B\bigr)
=\min\{y_3,S_3-y_3,y_4,S_4-y_4\}.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)=S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]

For the lower bound, choose a smooth function \(\rho:B\to\mathbb R\) with \(\rho|_{\partial B}=0\), \(|\nabla \rho|\le 1+\varepsilon\), and \(\rho(y)\ge \delta(y)-\varepsilon\). Define the \(2\)-form
\[
\alpha=\rho(x_3,x_4)\,dx_1\wedge dx_2.
\]
This form restricts to \(0\) on every face of \(\partial S\): on the \(x_1,x_2\)-faces because \(dx_1\wedge dx_2\) vanishes there, and on the \(x_3,x_4\)-faces because \(\rho=0\) on \(\partial B\).

If \(Y\) is a relative filling of \(P_y\), then \(\partial Y-P_y\) is supported in \(\partial S\), so Stokes gives
\[
\int_Y d\alpha=\int_{\partial Y}\alpha=\int_{P_y}\alpha=\rho(y)S_1S_2.
\]
Moreover
\[
d\alpha=d\rho\wedge dx_1\wedge dx_2
\]
has comass at most \(1+\varepsilon\). Hence
\[
(\delta(y)-\varepsilon)S_1S_2\le (1+\varepsilon)\mathbf M(Y).
\]
Letting \(\varepsilon\to 0\) yields
\[
\mathbf M(Y)\ge S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]

For the upper bound, take a shortest segment \(\gamma\) in \(B\) from \(y\) to \(\partial B\). Then
\[
[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times \gamma
\]
is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\), with mass \(S_1S_2\,\ell(\gamma)=S_1S_2\,\delta(y)\).

So the formula is exact.

In particular, if
\[
y_3\in[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\quad\text{and}\quad y_4\in[S_4/3,2S_4/3],
\]
then, because \(S_4\ge S_3\),
\[
\delta(y)\ge S_3/3,
\]
and hence
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)\ge \frac13 S_1S_2S_3.
\]

But with only \(y_3\) central, no such uniform lower bound is true. Indeed, if \(y_4=\varepsilon\), then the prism
\[
[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y_3\}\times[0,\varepsilon]
\]
fills \(P_y\) relative to \(\partial S\) and has mass \(S_1S_2\varepsilon\).

## 4. Coarea estimate

For a.e. \(y\in B\), the slice \(z_y=\langle \llbracket R\rrbracket,F,y\rangle\) exists, and for good regular values it agrees with the smooth fiber \(F^{-1}(y)\). The coarea formula gives
\[
\int_B \operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_R \operatorname{Jac}_2 F(x)\,dx.
\]
Since \(F=\pi\circ f\) and \(\pi\) is an orthogonal projection,
\[
\operatorname{Jac}_2 F(x)=|\Lambda^2(d\pi\circ df_x)|
\le |\Lambda^2 df_x|
\le \operatorname{Dil}_2(f)
\le 1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\int_{[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\le \operatorname{Vol}(R).
\]

## 5. Conditional central-base proposition

Let
\[
B_c=[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\times[0,S_4].
\]
If for a.e. \(y\in B_c\),
\[
\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\ge a\,S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3},
\]
then by coarea,
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)
\ge \int_{B_c}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\ge aS_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}\cdot \frac{S_3S_4}{3}.
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)\ge \frac a3\,S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]

More generally, if the same area lower bound holds on a measurable subset \(E\subset B_c\) with
\[
|E|\ge \theta |B_c|
\]
for some fixed \(\theta>0\), then
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)\ge \frac{\theta a}{3}\,S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]
For example, the doubly central base
\[
[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\times[S_4/3,2S_4/3]
\]
has \(\theta=1/3\) relative to \(B_c\).

Piecewise smooth maps are handled directly in this current framework: slices exist for a.e. \(y\), the boundary and pushforward identities hold for good regular values on the smooth strata, and coarea is obtained by summing over the finitely many smooth pieces. No smoothing is needed.

## 1. The suspected pointwise counterexample is valid

Normalize \(S_1=S_2=1,\ S_3=q,\ S_4=L\). With

\[
r=b^{1/4}\delta^{-1/2}q^{-1/4},\qquad 
t=\frac{\sqrt b\,q^{1/2}}r=b^{1/4}\delta^{1/2}q^{3/4},
\]

and

\[
R_1=r,\quad R_2=R_3=t,\quad R_4=\frac{\delta qL}{t},
\]

we get

\[
R_3R_4=\delta qL,
\]

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)=r\cdot t\cdot t\cdot \frac{\delta qL}{t}
=\delta\sqrt b\,q^{3/2}L,
\]

\[
R_1^2R_2R_3=r^2t^2=bq,
\]

and

\[
R_1^2\operatorname{Vol}(R)=bqL.
\]

For \(q\) large, \(R_1\le R_2=R_3\), and for \(L\gtrsim b^{1/2}q^{1/2}\), also \(R_3\le R_4\).

Now take a circle \(\gamma\) of radius

\[
\rho \sim \sqrt{\frac q r}
\]

in the \(x_2x_3\)-plane, centered far from the boundary. Since

\[
\frac{t}{\rho}
\sim b^{3/8}\delta^{1/4}q^{1/8}\to\infty,
\]

the circle fits for \(q\) large. Let

\[
z=[0,r]\times \gamma
\]

at a fixed central \(x_4\). Then \(z\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle: its boundary lies in the faces \(x_1=0,r\). Its area is

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z)\sim r\rho
=\sqrt{rq}
=b^{1/8}\delta^{-1/4}q^{3/8}
=o(\sqrt q).
\]

But the natural filling \([0,r]\times D_\gamma\) has volume

\[
\sim r\rho^2\sim q.
\]

A matching lower bound follows by slicing any relative filling \(Y\) by \(x_1=s\). For a.e. \(s\), the slice \(Y_s\) is a relative \(2\)-chain in the \(x_2x_3x_4\)-box filling \(\gamma\). Since \(\gamma\) sits a distance \(\gtrsim \rho\) from the boundary, any such relative filling has area \(\gtrsim \rho^2\). Hence

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(Y)\ge \int_0^r c\rho^2\,ds\gtrsim r\rho^2\sim q.
\]

So the cylinder has

\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z)\sim q=S_1S_2S_3
\]

but

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z)\sim q^{3/8}\ll S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}=\sqrt q.
\]

Thus the naive pointwise lemma is false.

---

## 2. A pure Almgren-family lemma is also insufficient

Even adding “the cycles form a degree-one \(2\)-parameter family” is not enough unless one uses the fact that the family comes from an actual \(2\)-dilation map.

Here is a model family satisfying the numerical side constraints and the central filling lower bound, but with average area far below \(\sqrt q\).

Take \(S=(1,1,q,q)\), choose small \(\kappa\), and set

\[
R_1=\kappa,\qquad 
R_2=\kappa^{-4},\qquad 
R_3=q\kappa^2,\qquad 
R_4=\frac q\kappa,
\]

with \(q\ge \kappa^{-8}\). Then

\[
R_3R_4=q^2\kappa=\kappa S_3S_4,
\]

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)=\frac{q^2}{\kappa^2}
\le \kappa q^{5/2}
=\kappa S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]

for \(q^{1/2}\ge \kappa^{-3}\). Also the standard monomial estimates are saturated or satisfied:

\[
R_1R_2=\kappa^{-3}\ge 1,
\]

\[
R_1R_2R_3=\frac q\kappa\ge q,
\]

\[
R_1^2R_2R_3=q,
\]

\[
R_1^2\operatorname{Vol}(R)=q^2=S_1^3S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Define a family of relative \(2\)-cycles parameterized by \((u,v)\in [0,q]^2\):

\[
z_{u,v}=[0,R_1]\times [0,R_2]\times 
\left\{\frac{R_3}{q}u\right\}
\times
\left\{\frac{R_4}{q}v\right\}.
\]

This is an Almgren degree-one sweepout of \(R\). Each cycle has area

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z_{u,v})=R_1R_2=\kappa^{-3}.
\]

For central \((u,v)\), the distance in the \(x_3x_4\)-rectangle to the boundary is \(\gtrsim R_3\). A double slicing argument gives

\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_{u,v})
\gtrsim R_1R_2R_3
=\frac q\kappa
\gtrsim q.
\]

Indeed, slice a filling first by \(x_1\), then slice the resulting \(2\)-chain by \(x_2\). Each \(x_2\)-slice must connect the point \((x_3,x_4)\) to the boundary of the \(x_3x_4\)-rectangle, so has length \(\gtrsim R_3\). Integrating gives the lower bound.

But for \(q\gg \kappa^{-6}\),

\[
\kappa^{-3}\ll \sqrt q.
\]

Therefore no argument depending only on:

1. Almgren degree-one family,
2. central filling lower bound,
3. the standard monomial side estimates,

can prove the desired theorem. The proof must use the extra structure that the family is made of actual fibers of a degree-one map with \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\).

---

## 3. A structural lemma for actual fibers

Let

\[
F=(f_3,f_4):R\to [0,S_3]\times[0,S_4],
\qquad 
G=(f_1,f_2):R\to [0,S_1]\times[0,S_2].
\]

For a regular value \(y\), the fiber

\[
z_y=F^{-1}(y)
\]

is a relative \(2\)-cycle, and \(G|_{z_y}\) has relative degree one onto the \(S_1\times S_2\) fiber. Hence

\[
\int_{z_y} J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\,dA \ge S_1S_2.
\]

At a regular point \(x\in z_y\), let

\[
\sigma_1(x)\ge \sigma_2(x)>0
\]

be the singular values of \(dF_x\) on \(T_xz_y^\perp\). Then the \(2\)-dilation bound implies

\[
J_2(G|_{T_xz_y})\le \sigma_1(x)^{-2}.
\]

Proof: choose a unit normal vector \(n\) with \(|dF(n)|=\sigma_1\). For every unit \(v\in T_xz_y\), we have \(dF(v)=0\). The \(G(v)\wedge F(n)\) component of \(df(v)\wedge df(n)\) has norm \(|dG(v)|\sigma_1\). Since \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\), this is \(\le 1\). Thus \(|dG(v)|\le \sigma_1^{-1}\), so the \(2\)-Jacobian of \(G\) on the fiber tangent plane is at most \(\sigma_1^{-2}\).

Therefore, for every regular central fiber,

\[
\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA \ge S_1S_2.
\]

Coarea gives

\[
\int_{B_0}\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\sigma_1^{-2}J_2F\,dx
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx,
\]

where \(B_0\subset [0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]\) is a fixed central subrectangle. Hence

\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx
\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Meanwhile,

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}J_2F\,dx
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1\sigma_2\,dx.
\]

Writing

\[
d\mu=\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx,
\]

we have

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu,
\]

and

\[
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0))
\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Thus the original theorem would follow from the following precise missing estimate:

\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu
\gtrsim
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}\,
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0)).
\]

Equivalently, the actual fibers must carry enough average maximal base-stretch:

\[
\langle \sigma_1^2\rangle_{\mu}
\gtrsim
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}.
\]

If this holds, then

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\gtrsim
S_1S_2S_3S_4
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}
=
S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]

and since

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\le \operatorname{Vol}(R),
\]

we get the desired volume lower bound.

So the hard remaining point is not a pointwise isoperimetric statement about arbitrary cycles. It is a mixed-Jacobian / solid-torus statement forcing the base map \(F=(f_3,f_4)\) to have enough \(\mu\)-average maximal stretch on the degree-carrying part of the fibers. The abstract counterfamilies above evade this because they do not come with coordinate functions \(f_1,f_2,f_3,f_4\) satisfying all mixed \(2\)-Jacobian bounds.

---

## 4. Status

The strongest clean progress is:

- the proposed pointwise counterexample is valid;
- a purely Almgren-complex family lemma is false, even under the standard side inequalities;
- actual fibers satisfy a strong mixed-Jacobian inequality absent from arbitrary families;
- the original theorem is reduced to a precise “base-stretch dispersion” lemma, which is the natural place for the solid-torus / Steenrod-square mechanism.

I focused on the **base-stretch measure** itself. The main useful conclusion is that the raw measure  
\[
d\mu=\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx
\]
is not quite the right degree-carrying object unless one first removes zero-degree sheets. There is a stronger and cleaner measure-selection reduction that still implies the theorem.

Let
\[
A=F^{-1}(B_0),\qquad a=\sigma_1,\quad b=\sigma_2,\qquad J_F=ab,
\]
and set
\[
\lambda:=\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}\ge 1.
\]

## 1. A sharper mixed-Jacobian inequality on fibers

For a regular fiber \(z_y=F^{-1}(y)\), let \(T_xz_y=\ker dF_x\). Let \(\alpha_1\ge \alpha_2\) be the singular values of \(dG\) on \(T_xz_y\). Besides the estimate from t2,
\[
J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\le a^{-2},
\]
one also has the pointwise bound
\[
J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\le 1.
\]
Indeed, if \(v,w\in T_xz_y\), then \(dF(v)=dF(w)=0\), so
\[
|df(v)\wedge df(w)|=|dG(v)\wedge dG(w)|\le 1.
\]
Also, choosing a unit normal vector \(n\) with \(|dF(n)|=a\), the mixed component
\[
dG(v)\wedge dF(n)
\]
inside \(df(v)\wedge df(n)\) gives \(|dG(v)|a\le 1\), hence \(\alpha_1\le a^{-1}\). Therefore
\[
J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\le \min(1,a^{-2}).
\]

## 2. The natural degree-carrying measure

Define
\[
d\nu:=\min(1,a^{-2})\,J_F\,dx
      = b\min(a,a^{-1})\,dx.
\]
Then
\[
d\nu=
\begin{cases}
ab\,dx=J_F\,dx,& a\le 1,\\[2mm]
\dfrac ba\,dx=d\mu,& a\ge 1.
\end{cases}
\]

For every nonnegative test function \(\Phi\) on
\[
P\times B_0,\qquad P=[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2],
\]
one has
\[
\int_A \Phi(G(x),F(x))\,d\nu(x)
\ge
\int_{B_0}\int_P \Phi(p,y)\,dp\,dy.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
f_\#\nu \ge \mathcal L^4\!\lfloor(P\times B_0).
\]

This follows by applying the oriented area formula on each regular fiber \(z_y\). Since \(G|_{z_y}\) has relative degree one onto \(P\),
\[
\int_{z_y}\phi(G)\,J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\,dA\ge \int_P \phi(p)\,dp.
\]
Using \(J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\le \min(1,a^{-2})\) and then coarea for \(F\) gives the claim.

A crucial identity is
\[
\int_A J_F\,dx
=
\int_A \max(1,a^2)\,d\nu.
\]
Indeed, on \(\{a\le1\}\), \(d\nu=J_Fdx\); on \(\{a\ge1\}\), \(d\nu=(b/a)dx\), so \(J_Fdx=a^2d\nu\).

Thus the energy we need to lower-bound is exactly the \(\max(1,\sigma_1^2)\)-moment of the degree-carrying measure \(\nu\).

In particular,
\[
\int_A J_F\,dx\ge \nu(A)\ge c\,S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]
This already rules out the contradiction hypotheses when \(\lambda\lesssim \epsilon^{-1}\). The genuinely hard case is therefore
\[
\lambda=\sqrt{S_3/S_2}\gg \epsilon^{-1}.
\]

## 3. Corrected dispersion lemma that would imply the theorem

Because \(f_\#\nu\ge \mathcal L^4\!\lfloor(P\times B_0)\), choose a submeasure \(\nu_0\le\nu\) with
\[
f_\#\nu_0=\mathcal L^4\!\lfloor(P\times B_0).
\]
For example, if \(m=f_\#\nu\), set
\[
\theta=\frac{d\mathcal L^4\!\lfloor(P\times B_0)}{dm}\le1,
\qquad
d\nu_0=(\theta\circ f)\,d\nu.
\]
Then
\[
\nu_0(A)=|P||B_0|\sim S_1S_2S_3S_4,
\]
and
\[
\int_A J_F\,dx
\ge
\int_A \max(1,a^2)\,d\nu_0.
\]

So the theorem would follow from the following sharper replacement for the raw \(\mu\)-dispersion statement:

> **Corrected low-stretch exclusion.** There are dimensional constants \(c,C>0\) such that, under  
> \[
> R_1\le \epsilon S_1,\qquad R_3R_4\le \epsilon S_3S_4,
> \]
> every degree-carrying submeasure \(\nu_0\) satisfies
> \[
> \nu_0\{a\le L\}
> \le
> C\left(\epsilon+\frac{L^2}{\lambda}\right)\nu_0(A)
> \]
> for \(1\le L\le c\lambda^{1/2}\).

Indeed, choose \(L^2=c_0\lambda\) with \(c_0>0\) small and then \(\epsilon>0\) small. The estimate gives
\[
\nu_0\{a\le L\}\le \frac12\nu_0(A).
\]
Hence
\[
\int_A J_F\,dx
\ge
\int_{\{a>L\}} a^2\,d\nu_0
\ge
\frac12 L^2\nu_0(A)
\gtrsim
\lambda S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]
Since
\[
\lambda S_1S_2S_3S_4
=
S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]
this gives the desired volume lower bound.

So the precise remaining task is not to prove dispersion for all of \(\mu\), but to prove that a degree-carrying sheet \(\nu_0\) cannot mostly live where \(\sigma_1^2\ll \sqrt{S_3/S_2}\).

## 4. Why the raw \(\mu\)-formulation is too strong

On \(\{a\ge1\}\), \(\nu=\mu\). But on \(\{a<1\}\),
\[
d\mu=\frac ba\,dx
=
a^{-2}d\nu,
\]
so \(\mu\) can count large amounts of low-stretch, zero-degree or canceling material that does not help cover \(P\times B_0\). The theorem only needs a lower bound on the energy carried by one degree-one sheet over \(P\times B_0\). Thus \(\nu_0\), not total \(\mu\), is the stable object.

This is not merely cosmetic: adding low-stretch null sheets can greatly increase total \(\mu\) while contributing very little to \(\int a^2\,d\mu\). A proof using total \(\mu\) would first need a separate argument ruling out such sheets under the side assumptions.

## 5. A useful dual-fiber lower bound

There is also a symmetric slicing statement that may be useful for the missing low-stretch exclusion.

Let
\[
P_0=[S_1/3,2S_1/3]\times [S_2/3,2S_2/3].
\]
For a good regular \(p\in P_0\), set
\[
\Sigma_p:=G^{-1}(p).
\]
Then \(\Sigma_p\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \(R\), and by the same cap-product argument as in t1,
\[
F_\#\Sigma_p=[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]
\]
as a relative current in the target slice \(\{p\}\times[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]\).

Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(\Sigma_p)
\ge
c\,S_1S_3S_4.
\]
Indeed, any relative filling of \(\Sigma_p\) pushes forward to a relative filling of the base rectangle
\[
B_p:=\{p\}\times[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]
\]
inside \(S\), and \(\operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\le1\). The exact target filling volume is
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(B_p)
=
S_3S_4\,\operatorname{dist}(p,\partial P),
\]
which is \(\gtrsim S_1S_3S_4\) for \(p\in P_0\).

This dual lower bound alone does not prove the theorem, because one still needs an upper-filling or width estimate for these actual dual fibers. But it identifies another equivalent place where the missing solid-torus/Steenrod-type obstruction can enter.

can hold when \(S_3/S_2\) is large. I use Guth’s standard mod-2 relative flat cycle/minimax convention: \(V(\alpha)\) is the infimum over families detecting \(\alpha\), and Steenrod classes are defined on these cycle spaces. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/math/0702066))

---

## 1. The cheap two-sheet family detecting \(Sq_1 a\)

Collapse \(\partial B\) to a basepoint, so \(B/\partial B\simeq S^2\). Consider the space \(X\) of mod-2 relative \(0\)-cycles in \(B\) represented by at most two points. Concretely,

\[
X=\operatorname{Sym}^2(S^2)/\Delta(S^2),
\]

where \(\Delta(S^2)\) is collapsed because two coincident mod-2 points cancel.

Now \(\operatorname{Sym}^2(S^2)\cong \mathbb CP^2\), and the diagonal is the conic representing \(2h\in H_2(\mathbb CP^2;\mathbb Z)\). Therefore the quotient \(X=\mathbb CP^2/\Delta\) satisfies

\[
H_2(X;\mathbb Z)\cong \mathbb Z/2,\qquad H^2(X;\mathbb F_2)\cong \mathbb F_2,\qquad H^3(X;\mathbb F_2)\cong \mathbb F_2.
\]

Let \(u\in H^2(X;\mathbb F_2)\) be the generator. The Bockstein \(H^2(X;\mathbb F_2)\to H^3(X;\mathbb Z)\) is nonzero, hence

\[
Sq^1u\neq 0.
\]

The inclusion \(j:X\to Z_0(B;\mathbb F_2)\) satisfies

\[
j^*a_B=u,
\]

because the one-point subfamily \(S^2\to X\), \(p\mapsto\{p,*\}\), is the usual sweepout of \(B\). Hence

\[
j^*(Sq^1 a_B)=Sq^1u\neq 0.
\]

So \(X\) detects \(Sq_1 a_B\) using only \(0\)-cycles of cardinality at most \(2\).

---

## 2. Cross with the small \(S_1\times S_2\) rectangle

Define

\[
\Phi:Z_0(B;\mathbb F_2)\longrightarrow Z_2(S;\mathbb F_2)
\]

by

\[
\Phi(p_1+\cdots+p_m)
=
\sum_{i=1}^m \llbracket A\times\{p_i\}\rrbracket.
\]

This is continuous in the flat topology: if two base points collide, the two sheets cancel, and the flat filling is \(A\) times a short segment. Also,

\[
\mathbf M(\Phi(c))\le (\#c)\,S_1S_2.
\]

For \(c\in X\), \(\#c\le 2\), hence

\[
\mathbf M(\Phi(c))\le 2S_1S_2.
\]

Moreover, \(\Phi^*a_S=a_B\), since crossing a \(0\)-cycle sweepout of \(B\) with \(A\) gives the corresponding \(2\)-cycle sweepout of \(S=A\times B\). Therefore

\[
(\Phi\circ j)^*(Sq^1a_S)
=
Sq^1(j^*\Phi^*a_S)
=
Sq^1u
\neq 0.
\]

Thus \(\Phi\circ j:X\to Z_2(S)\) detects \(Sq_1a_S\), while every cycle in the family has area at most \(2S_1S_2\). Consequently,

\[
\boxed{V_S(Sq_1a_S)\le 2S_1S_2.}
\]

So \(Sq_1a\) cannot produce the desired \(S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}\) target lower bound.

---

## 3. What this says about the t2 coordinate-sheet counterfamily

The original two-parameter coordinate-sheet family from t2 detects only \(a\). But its **two-point enlargement** detects \(Sq_1a\).

For a source rectangle \(R=A_R\times B_R\), with

\[
A_R=[0,R_1]\times[0,R_2],\qquad B_R=[0,R_3]\times[0,R_4],
\]

the family

\[
c\longmapsto A_R\times c,\qquad c\in Z_0^{\le 2}(B_R),
\]

detects \(Sq_1a_R\) and has area at most

\[
2R_1R_2.
\]

In the t2 model with \(S=(1,1,q,q)\) and

\[
R_1=\kappa,\qquad R_2=\kappa^{-4},
\]

this gives a \(Sq_1a_R\)-detecting family with maximal area

\[
2R_1R_2=2\kappa^{-3}.
\]

For \(q\gg \kappa^{-6}\),

\[
2\kappa^{-3}\ll \sqrt q=S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}.
\]

So the “coordinate sheets detect only \(a\), not \(Sq_1a\)” distinction is not enough: coordinate sheets detect \(Sq_1a\) as soon as we allow two mod-2 sheets.

---

## 4. Why the solid-torus attempt also does not give a cycle-space minimax lower bound

The proposed target cycles

\[
[0,S_1]\times \gamma\subset [0,S_1]\times [0,S_2]\times[0,S_3]\times\{x_4\}
\]

can indeed have area \(\sim S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}\) if \(\gamma\) is a loop of length \(\sim\sqrt{S_2S_3}\). But if the only topological class detected is \(Sq_1a\), then the two-sheet family above replaces it with cycles of area \(2S_1S_2\). Thus the high-area solid-torus representative is not minimax-forced in the ordinary cycle space.

There is also a functorial problem: \(2\)-dilation controls pushforwards of \(2\)-surfaces, not inverse images of chosen target surfaces. The central fibers \(P_y\) from t1 are special because they arise by capping with the point class in the \((x_3,x_4)\)-base, giving a degree-one pushforward identity. A surface like \([0,S_1]\times\gamma\) is not a fiber of that projection, and as a relative \(2\)-cycle in a \(4\)-ball it is nullhomologous. Degree one alone does not force its preimage to map onto it with degree one.

So a viable “solid torus” proof would need an additional invariant: not just a class in the ordinary relative \(2\)-cycle space, but something that remembers **fillings, linking, or the actual fiber structure of \(F=(f_3,f_4)\)**.

---

## 5. What stronger invariant seems necessary

The failure above is structural. Ordinary mod-2 cycle-space cohomology is blind to the splitting of a surface into several small vertical sheets. The cheap family

\[
c\mapsto [0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times c
\]

is exactly the mechanism by which Steenrod operations become inexpensive.

Thus the missing obstruction probably has to live in one of these stronger settings:

1. **A filtered cycle-filling space**, where a cycle is considered together with a chosen relative \(3\)-filling, so that large filling volume cannot be ignored.

2. **A decorated cycle space of degree-one sheets**, excluding multi-sheet mod-2 cancellation. But such a space is not stable under ordinary flat minimax deformations.

3. **The actual-fiber mixed-Jacobian structure from t2**, namely the fact that fibers of \(F=(f_3,f_4)\) carry degree one under \(G=(f_1,f_2)\) and satisfy the pointwise mixed inequality involving \(\sigma_1^{-2}\). This is not captured by arbitrary Almgren families or ordinary Steenrod operations.

In particular, \(Sq_1a\) is not the missing invariant.

## Task t1 — Rigorous central-fiber/coarea reduction
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Suppose $(M, g)$ and $(N,h)$ are piecewise smooth Riemannian manifolds. Below, \(\llbracket A\rrbracket\) denotes the integral current carried by an oriented rectangle \(A\). I will use the integral-current model for relative chains:
\[
T\in Z_k(M,\partial M)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \partial T\text{ is supported in }\partial M,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_M(T)=\inf\{\mathbf M(Y):\ Y\in \mathbf I_{k+1}(M),\ \partial Y-T\in \mathbf I_k(\partial M)\}.
\]

There is one necessary correction: item **(3)** as stated is false if only \(y_3\) is central. A slice \(P_y\) with \(y_4\) very close to \(0\) can be filled by a thin prism to the face \(x_4=0\). The correct target estimate is
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)=S_1S_2\,\operatorname{dist}\bigl((y_3,y_4),\partial([0,S_3]\times[0,S_4])\bigr).
\]
Thus the desired lower bound \( \gtrsim S_1S_2S_3\) holds on the doubly central sub-base, hence on a fixed positive fraction of the \(y_3\)-central base.

## 1. The fiber is a relative cycle and pushes to \(P_y\)

Let
\[
B=[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4],\qquad \pi:S\to B,\qquad F=\pi\circ f.
\]
Assume first that \(y\in \operatorname{int}B\) is a good regular value of \(F\), i.e. regular on all smooth strata of \(R\) and \(\partial R\). Then
\[
z_y:=F^{-1}(y)
\]
is an oriented compact surface with boundary contained in \(\partial R\). Equivalently, as a current,
\[
\partial z_y\in \mathbf I_1(\partial R),
\]
so \(z_y\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \((R,\partial R)\).

Because \(F=\pi\circ f\), every point of \(z_y\) maps into
\[
\pi^{-1}(y)=P_y=[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y_3\}\times\{y_4\}.
\]
Also \(f(\partial R)\subset \partial S\), so
\[
\operatorname{spt}\partial(f_\#z_y)\subset P_y\cap\partial S=\partial P_y.
\]
Thus \(f_\#z_y\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \((P_y,\partial P_y)\).

Now let \(u_y\in H^2(B,B\setminus\{y\};\mathbb Z)\) be the local orientation class of \(B\) at \(y\). By Poincare-Lefschetz cap product and excision,
\[
[z_y]=[R,\partial R]\frown F^*u_y.
\]
Since \(F=\pi\circ f\) and \(f\) has degree one as a map of pairs,
\[
\begin{aligned}
f_*[z_y]
&=f_*\bigl([R,\partial R]\frown f^*\pi^*u_y\bigr)\\
&=f_*[R,\partial R]\frown \pi^*u_y\\
&=[S,\partial S]\frown \pi^*u_y\\
&=[P_y,\partial P_y].
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore \(f_\#z_y\) represents the fundamental relative class of \(P_y\).

In current language this gives actual equality. Indeed, \(f_\#z_y\) is an integral \(2\)-current supported in the connected \(2\)-rectangle \(P_y\), and its boundary is supported in \(\partial P_y\). By the constancy theorem,
\[
f_\#z_y=m\,\llbracket P_y\rrbracket
\]
for some \(m\in\mathbb Z\). The cap-product computation gives \(m=1\). Hence
\[
f_\#z_y=\llbracket P_y\rrbracket
\]
as an integral current in \(S\), and therefore certainly as a relative chain modulo \(\partial S\).

## 2. Fillings push forward, and \(\operatorname{Dil}_2\le 1\) gives the filling inequality

Let \(Y\in \mathbf I_3(R)\) be a relative filling of \(z_y\), so
\[
\partial Y-z_y\in \mathbf I_2(\partial R).
\]
Pushing forward,
\[
\partial(f_\#Y)-f_\#z_y=f_\#(\partial Y-z_y)
\]
is supported in \(f(\partial R)\subset\partial S\). Since \(f_\#z_y=P_y\), we get
\[
\partial(f_\#Y)-P_y\in \mathbf I_2(\partial S).
\]
Thus \(f_\#Y\) is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\).

At a differentiability point of \(f\), let
\[
\sigma_1\ge \sigma_2\ge \sigma_3\ge \sigma_4\ge 0
\]
be the singular values of \(df\). The assumption \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\) means \(\sigma_1\sigma_2\le 1\). Hence \(\sigma_2\le 1\), so also \(\sigma_3\le 1\), and therefore
\[
\sigma_1\sigma_2\sigma_3\le 1.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\le 1\). Consequently,
\[
\mathbf M(f_\#Y)\le \operatorname{Dil}_3(f)\,\mathbf M(Y)\le \mathbf M(Y).
\]
Taking the infimum over all relative fillings \(Y\) of \(z_y\),
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_y)\ge \operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y).
\]

## 3. Correct filling estimate in \(S\)

Let
\[
\delta(y):=\operatorname{dist}\bigl(y,\partial B\bigr)
=\min\{y_3,S_3-y_3,y_4,S_4-y_4\}.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)=S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]

For the lower bound, choose a smooth function \(\rho:B\to\mathbb R\) with \(\rho|_{\partial B}=0\), \(|\nabla \rho|\le 1+\varepsilon\), and \(\rho(y)\ge \delta(y)-\varepsilon\). Define the \(2\)-form
\[
\alpha=\rho(x_3,x_4)\,dx_1\wedge dx_2.
\]
This form restricts to \(0\) on every face of \(\partial S\): on the \(x_1,x_2\)-faces because \(dx_1\wedge dx_2\) vanishes there, and on the \(x_3,x_4\)-faces because \(\rho=0\) on \(\partial B\).

If \(Y\) is a relative filling of \(P_y\), then \(\partial Y-P_y\) is supported in \(\partial S\), so Stokes gives
\[
\int_Y d\alpha=\int_{\partial Y}\alpha=\int_{P_y}\alpha=\rho(y)S_1S_2.
\]
Moreover
\[
d\alpha=d\rho\wedge dx_1\wedge dx_2
\]
has comass at most \(1+\varepsilon\). Hence
\[
(\delta(y)-\varepsilon)S_1S_2\le (1+\varepsilon)\mathbf M(Y).
\]
Letting \(\varepsilon\to 0\) yields
\[
\mathbf M(Y)\ge S_1S_2\,\delta(y).
\]

For the upper bound, take a shortest segment \(\gamma\) in \(B\) from \(y\) to \(\partial B\). Then
\[
[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times \gamma
\]
is a relative filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\), with mass \(S_1S_2\,\ell(\gamma)=S_1S_2\,\delta(y)\).

So the formula is exact.

In particular, if
\[
y_3\in[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\quad\text{and}\quad y_4\in[S_4/3,2S_4/3],
\]
then, because \(S_4\ge S_3\),
\[
\delta(y)\ge S_3/3,
\]
and hence
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_S(P_y)\ge \frac13 S_1S_2S_3.
\]

But with only \(y_3\) central, no such uniform lower bound is true. Indeed, if \(y_4=\varepsilon\), then the prism
\[
[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y_3\}\times[0,\varepsilon]
\]
fills \(P_y\) relative to \(\partial S\) and has mass \(S_1S_2\varepsilon\).

## 4. Coarea estimate

For a.e. \(y\in B\), the slice \(z_y=\langle \llbracket R\rrbracket,F,y\rangle\) exists, and for good regular values it agrees with the smooth fiber \(F^{-1}(y)\). The coarea formula gives
\[
\int_B \operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_R \operatorname{Jac}_2 F(x)\,dx.
\]
Since \(F=\pi\circ f\) and \(\pi\) is an orthogonal projection,
\[
\operatorname{Jac}_2 F(x)=|\Lambda^2(d\pi\circ df_x)|
\le |\Lambda^2 df_x|
\le \operatorname{Dil}_2(f)
\le 1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\int_{[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\le \operatorname{Vol}(R).
\]

## 5. Conditional central-base proposition

Let
\[
B_c=[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\times[0,S_4].
\]
If for a.e. \(y\in B_c\),
\[
\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\ge a\,S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3},
\]
then by coarea,
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)
\ge \int_{B_c}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\ge aS_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}\cdot \frac{S_3S_4}{3}.
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)\ge \frac a3\,S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]

More generally, if the same area lower bound holds on a measurable subset \(E\subset B_c\) with
\[
|E|\ge \theta |B_c|
\]
for some fixed \(\theta>0\), then
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)\ge \frac{\theta a}{3}\,S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]
For example, the doubly central base
\[
[S_3/3,2S_3/3]\times[S_4/3,2S_4/3]
\]
has \(\theta=1/3\) relative to \(B_c\).

Piecewise smooth maps are handled directly in this current framework: slices exist for a.e. \(y\), the boundary and pushforward identities hold for good regular values on the smooth strata, and coarea is obtained by summing over the finitely many smooth pieces. No smoothing is needed.

## 1. The suspected pointwise counterexample is valid

Normalize \(S_1=S_2=1,\ S_3=q,\ S_4=L\). With

\[
r=b^{1/4}\delta^{-1/2}q^{-1/4},\qquad 
t=\frac{\sqrt b\,q^{1/2}}r=b^{1/4}\delta^{1/2}q^{3/4},
\]

and

\[
R_1=r,\quad R_2=R_3=t,\quad R_4=\frac{\delta qL}{t},
\]

we get

\[
R_3R_4=\delta qL,
\]

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)=r\cdot t\cdot t\cdot \frac{\delta qL}{t}
=\delta\sqrt b\,q^{3/2}L,
\]

\[
R_1^2R_2R_3=r^2t^2=bq,
\]

and

\[
R_1^2\operatorname{Vol}(R)=bqL.
\]

For \(q\) large, \(R_1\le R_2=R_3\), and for \(L\gtrsim b^{1/2}q^{1/2}\), also \(R_3\le R_4\).

Now take a circle \(\gamma\) of radius

\[
\rho \sim \sqrt{\frac q r}
\]

in the \(x_2x_3\)-plane, centered far from the boundary. Since

\[
\frac{t}{\rho}
\sim b^{3/8}\delta^{1/4}q^{1/8}\to\infty,
\]

the circle fits for \(q\) large. Let

\[
z=[0,r]\times \gamma
\]

at a fixed central \(x_4\). Then \(z\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle: its boundary lies in the faces \(x_1=0,r\). Its area is

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z)\sim r\rho
=\sqrt{rq}
=b^{1/8}\delta^{-1/4}q^{3/8}
=o(\sqrt q).
\]

But the natural filling \([0,r]\times D_\gamma\) has volume

\[
\sim r\rho^2\sim q.
\]

A matching lower bound follows by slicing any relative filling \(Y\) by \(x_1=s\). For a.e. \(s\), the slice \(Y_s\) is a relative \(2\)-chain in the \(x_2x_3x_4\)-box filling \(\gamma\). Since \(\gamma\) sits a distance \(\gtrsim \rho\) from the boundary, any such relative filling has area \(\gtrsim \rho^2\). Hence

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(Y)\ge \int_0^r c\rho^2\,ds\gtrsim r\rho^2\sim q.
\]

So the cylinder has

\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z)\sim q=S_1S_2S_3
\]

but

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z)\sim q^{3/8}\ll S_1\sqrt{S_2S_3}=\sqrt q.
\]

Thus the naive pointwise lemma is false.

---

## 2. A pure Almgren-family lemma is also insufficient

Even adding “the cycles form a degree-one \(2\)-parameter family” is not enough unless one uses the fact that the family comes from an actual \(2\)-dilation map.

Here is a model family satisfying the numerical side constraints and the central filling lower bound, but with average area far below \(\sqrt q\).

Take \(S=(1,1,q,q)\), choose small \(\kappa\), and set

\[
R_1=\kappa,\qquad 
R_2=\kappa^{-4},\qquad 
R_3=q\kappa^2,\qquad 
R_4=\frac q\kappa,
\]

with \(q\ge \kappa^{-8}\). Then

\[
R_3R_4=q^2\kappa=\kappa S_3S_4,
\]

\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R)=\frac{q^2}{\kappa^2}
\le \kappa q^{5/2}
=\kappa S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]

for \(q^{1/2}\ge \kappa^{-3}\). Also the standard monomial estimates are saturated or satisfied:

\[
R_1R_2=\kappa^{-3}\ge 1,
\]

\[
R_1R_2R_3=\frac q\kappa\ge q,
\]

\[
R_1^2R_2R_3=q,
\]

\[
R_1^2\operatorname{Vol}(R)=q^2=S_1^3S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Define a family of relative \(2\)-cycles parameterized by \((u,v)\in [0,q]^2\):

\[
z_{u,v}=[0,R_1]\times [0,R_2]\times 
\left\{\frac{R_3}{q}u\right\}
\times
\left\{\frac{R_4}{q}v\right\}.
\]

This is an Almgren degree-one sweepout of \(R\). Each cycle has area

\[
\operatorname{Area}(z_{u,v})=R_1R_2=\kappa^{-3}.
\]

For central \((u,v)\), the distance in the \(x_3x_4\)-rectangle to the boundary is \(\gtrsim R_3\). A double slicing argument gives

\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(z_{u,v})
\gtrsim R_1R_2R_3
=\frac q\kappa
\gtrsim q.
\]

Indeed, slice a filling first by \(x_1\), then slice the resulting \(2\)-chain by \(x_2\). Each \(x_2\)-slice must connect the point \((x_3,x_4)\) to the boundary of the \(x_3x_4\)-rectangle, so has length \(\gtrsim R_3\). Integrating gives the lower bound.

But for \(q\gg \kappa^{-6}\),

\[
\kappa^{-3}\ll \sqrt q.
\]

Therefore no argument depending only on:

1. Almgren degree-one family,
2. central filling lower bound,
3. the standard monomial side estimates,

can prove the desired theorem. The proof must use the extra structure that the family is made of actual fibers of a degree-one map with \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\).

---

## 3. A structural lemma for actual fibers

Let

\[
F=(f_3,f_4):R\to [0,S_3]\times[0,S_4],
\qquad 
G=(f_1,f_2):R\to [0,S_1]\times[0,S_2].
\]

For a regular value \(y\), the fiber

\[
z_y=F^{-1}(y)
\]

is a relative \(2\)-cycle, and \(G|_{z_y}\) has relative degree one onto the \(S_1\times S_2\) fiber. Hence

\[
\int_{z_y} J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\,dA \ge S_1S_2.
\]

At a regular point \(x\in z_y\), let

\[
\sigma_1(x)\ge \sigma_2(x)>0
\]

be the singular values of \(dF_x\) on \(T_xz_y^\perp\). Then the \(2\)-dilation bound implies

\[
J_2(G|_{T_xz_y})\le \sigma_1(x)^{-2}.
\]

Proof: choose a unit normal vector \(n\) with \(|dF(n)|=\sigma_1\). For every unit \(v\in T_xz_y\), we have \(dF(v)=0\). The \(G(v)\wedge F(n)\) component of \(df(v)\wedge df(n)\) has norm \(|dG(v)|\sigma_1\). Since \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le 1\), this is \(\le 1\). Thus \(|dG(v)|\le \sigma_1^{-1}\), so the \(2\)-Jacobian of \(G\) on the fiber tangent plane is at most \(\sigma_1^{-2}\).

Therefore, for every regular central fiber,

\[
\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA \ge S_1S_2.
\]

Coarea gives

\[
\int_{B_0}\int_{z_y}\sigma_1^{-2}\,dA\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\sigma_1^{-2}J_2F\,dx
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx,
\]

where \(B_0\subset [0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]\) is a fixed central subrectangle. Hence

\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}
\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx
\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Meanwhile,

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}J_2F\,dx
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1\sigma_2\,dx.
\]

Writing

\[
d\mu=\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx,
\]

we have

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
=
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu,
\]

and

\[
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0))
\gtrsim S_1S_2S_3S_4.
\]

Thus the original theorem would follow from the following precise missing estimate:

\[
\int_{F^{-1}(B_0)}\sigma_1^2\,d\mu
\gtrsim
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}\,
\mu(F^{-1}(B_0)).
\]

Equivalently, the actual fibers must carry enough average maximal base-stretch:

\[
\langle \sigma_1^2\rangle_{\mu}
\gtrsim
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}.
\]

If this holds, then

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\gtrsim
S_1S_2S_3S_4
\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}
=
S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]

and since

\[
\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy
\le \operatorname{Vol}(R),
\]

we get the desired volume lower bound.

So the hard remaining point is not a pointwise isoperimetric statement about arbitrary cycles. It is a mixed-Jacobian / solid-torus statement forcing the base map \(F=(f_3,f_4)\) to have enough \(\mu\)-average maximal stretch on the degree-carrying part of the fibers. The abstract counterfamilies above evade this because they do not come with coordinate functions \(f_1,f_2,f_3,f_4\) satisfying all mixed \(2\)-Jacobian bounds.

---

## 4. Status

The strongest clean progress is:

- the proposed pointwise counterexample is valid;
- a purely Almgren-complex family lemma is false, even under the standard side inequalities;
- actual fibers satisfy a strong mixed-Jacobian inequality absent from arbitrary families;
- the original theorem is reduced to a precise “base-stretch dispersion” lemma, which is the natural place for the solid-torus / Steenrod-square mechanism.

I focused on the **base-stretch measure** itself. The main useful conclusion is that the raw measure  
\[
d\mu=\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\,dx

is not quite the right degree-carrying object unless one first removes zero-degree sheets. There is a stronger and cleaner measure-selection reduction that still implies the theorem.

Let
\[
A=F^{-1}(B_0),\qquad a=\sigma_1,\quad b=\sigma_2,\qquad J_F=ab,
\]
and set
\[
\lambda:=\sqrt{\frac{S_3}{S_2}}\ge 1.
\]

## 1. A sharper mixed-Jacobian inequality on fibers

For a regular fiber \(z_y=F^{-1}(y)\), let \(T_xz_y=\ker dF_x\). Let \(\alpha_1\ge \alpha_2\) be the singular values of \(dG\) on \(T_xz_y\). Besides the estimate from t2,
\[
J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\le a^{-2},
\]
one also has the pointwise bound
\[
J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\le 1.
\]
Indeed, if \(v,w\in T_xz_y\), then \(dF(v)=dF(w)=0\), so
\[
|df(v)\wedge df(w)|=|dG(v)\wedge dG(w)|\le 1.
\]
Also, choosing a unit normal vector \(n\) with \(|dF(n)|=a\), the mixed component
\[
dG(v)\wedge dF(n)
\]
inside \(df(v)\wedge df(n)\) gives \(|dG(v)|a\le 1\), hence \(\alpha_1\le a^{-1}\). Therefore
\[
J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\le \min(1,a^{-2}).
\]

## 2. The natural degree-carrying measure

Define
\[
d\nu:=\min(1,a^{-2})\,J_F\,dx
      = b\min(a,a^{-1})\,dx.
\]
Then
\[
d\nu=
\begin{cases}
ab\,dx=J_F\,dx,& a\le 1,\\[2mm]
\dfrac ba\,dx=d\mu,& a\ge 1.
\end{cases}
\]

For every nonnegative test function \(\Phi\) on
\[
P\times B_0,\qquad P=[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2],
\]
one has
\[
\int_A \Phi(G(x),F(x))\,d\nu(x)
\ge
\int_{B_0}\int_P \Phi(p,y)\,dp\,dy.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
f_\#\nu \ge \mathcal L^4\!\lfloor(P\times B_0).
\]

This follows by applying the oriented area formula on each regular fiber \(z_y\). Since \(G|_{z_y}\) has relative degree one onto \(P\),
\[
\int_{z_y}\phi(G)\,J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\,dA\ge \int_P \phi(p)\,dp.
\]
Using \(J_2(G|_{Tz_y})\le \min(1,a^{-2})\) and then coarea for \(F\) gives the claim.

A crucial identity is
\[
\int_A J_F\,dx
=
\int_A \max(1,a^2)\,d\nu.
\]
Indeed, on \(\{a\le1\}\), \(d\nu=J_Fdx\); on \(\{a\ge1\}\), \(d\nu=(b/a)dx\), so \(J_Fdx=a^2d\nu\).

Thus the energy we need to lower-bound is exactly the \(\max(1,\sigma_1^2)\)-moment of the degree-carrying measure \(\nu\).

In particular,
\[
\int_A J_F\,dx\ge \nu(A)\ge c\,S_1S_2S_3S_4.

This already rules out the contradiction hypotheses when \(\lambda\lesssim \epsilon^{-1}\). The genuinely hard case is therefore
\[
\lambda=\sqrt{S_3/S_2}\gg \epsilon^{-1}.
\]

## 3. Corrected dispersion lemma that would imply the theorem

Because \(f_\#\nu\ge \mathcal L^4\!\lfloor(P\times B_0)\), choose a submeasure \(\nu_0\le\nu\) with
\[
f_\#\nu_0=\mathcal L^4\!\lfloor(P\times B_0).
\]
For example, if \(m=f_\#\nu\), set
\[
\theta=\frac{d\mathcal L^4\!\lfloor(P\times B_0)}{dm}\le1,
\qquad
d\nu_0=(\theta\circ f)\,d\nu.
\]
Then
\[
\nu_0(A)=|P||B_0|\sim S_1S_2S_3S_4,
\]
and
\[
\int_A J_F\,dx
\ge
\int_A \max(1,a^2)\,d\nu_0.
\]

So the theorem would follow from the following sharper replacement for the raw \(\mu\)-dispersion statement:

> **Corrected low-stretch exclusion.** There are dimensional constants \(c,C>0\) such that, under  
> \[
> R_1\le \epsilon S_1,\qquad R_3R_4\le \epsilon S_3S_4,
> \]
> every degree-carrying submeasure \(\nu_0\) satisfies
> \[
> \nu_0\{a\le L\}
> \le
> C\left(\epsilon+\frac{L^2}{\lambda}\right)\nu_0(A)
> \]
> for \(1\le L\le c\lambda^{1/2}\).

Indeed, choose \(L^2=c_0\lambda\) with \(c_0>0\) small and then \(\epsilon>0\) small. The estimate gives
\[
\nu_0\{a\le L\}\le \frac12\nu_0(A).
\]
Hence
\[
\int_A J_F\,dx
\ge
\int_{\{a>L\}} a^2\,d\nu_0
\ge
\frac12 L^2\nu_0(A)
\gtrsim
\lambda S_1S_2S_3S_4.

Since
\[
\lambda S_1S_2S_3S_4
=
S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4,
\]
this gives the desired volume lower bound.

So the precise remaining task is not to prove dispersion for all of \(\mu\), but to prove that a degree-carrying sheet \(\nu_0\) cannot mostly live where \(\sigma_1^2\ll \sqrt{S_3/S_2}\).

## 4. Why the raw \(\mu\)-formulation is too strong

On \(\{a\ge1\}\), \(\nu=\mu\). But on \(\{a<1\}\),
\[
d\mu=\frac ba\,dx
=
a^{-2}d\nu,
\]
so \(\mu\) can count large amounts of low-stretch, zero-degree or canceling material that does not help cover \(P\times B_0\). The theorem only needs a lower bound on the energy carried by one degree-one sheet over \(P\times B_0\). Thus \(\nu_0\), not total \(\mu\), is the stable object.

This is not merely cosmetic: adding low-stretch null sheets can greatly increase total \(\mu\) while contributing very little to \(\int a^2\,d\mu\). A proof using total \(\mu\) would first need a separate argument ruling out such sheets under the side assumptions.

## 5. A useful dual-fiber lower bound

\]
Then \(\Sigma_p\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \(R\), and by the same cap-product argument as in t1,
\[
F_\#\Sigma_p=[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]
\]
as a relative current in the target slice \(\{p\}\times[0,S_3]\times[0,S_4]\).

Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{FillVol}_R(\Sigma_p)
\ge
c\,S_1S_3S_4.
\]
Indeed, any relative filling of \(\Sigma_p\) pushes forward to a relative filling of the base rectangle
